title: Automatic DNS Failover to a DR Region
description:
## Reacting Automatically to a Primary-Region Outage

A company hosts its application in a primary Region behind an Application Load Balancer, with a warm-standby copy and ALB in a DR Region. They need DNS to **automatically redirect user traffic to the DR Region's endpoint when the primary becomes unhealthy**, and to fail back automatically when the primary recovers — with **no human intervention** and minimal delay. They control the public domain in Amazon Route 53.

Which Route 53 configuration BEST meets these requirements?

options.text: Failover routing policy with a health check on the primary endpoint (primary + secondary records) and a low record TTL
Weighted routing splitting traffic 90/10 between the primary and DR endpoints
Simple routing to the primary, and have an on-call engineer change the record to the DR endpoint during an outage
Latency-based routing between the two Regions so users always hit the lower-latency endpoint

explanation: Route 53 **failover routing** with a **health check** is designed for active-passive DR: you create a PRIMARY record (associated with a health check on the primary endpoint) and a SECONDARY record for the DR endpoint. While the health check passes, Route 53 returns the primary; when it fails, Route 53 automatically returns the secondary — and reverts when the primary becomes healthy again. A **low TTL** ensures resolvers pick up the change quickly. **Weighted routing** (B) splits traffic by static weights and does not react to health, so it would keep sending ~90% of users to a dead primary. **Manual DNS change** (C) violates the "no human intervention" requirement and is slow/error-prone. **Latency-based routing** (D) optimizes for latency, not availability; it does react to health checks somewhat but is the wrong policy for deliberate active-passive failover and would send traffic to both Regions in normal operation rather than keeping DR passive.

hints.title: Which policy is health-aware by design?
hints.body: Active-passive DR with automatic redirect on failure maps to one specific Route 53 routing policy paired with health checks.

hints.title: Why low TTL?
hints.body: Even with automatic failover, clients cache DNS for the TTL. A low TTL shortens how long users stay pointed at the failed endpoint.
